title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 1
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: A national education authority has asked you to write a report on how media literacy should be integrated into the secondary school curriculum. Your report should identify the key skills students need, explain why early education is essential, and recommend specific practical measures that schools can implement.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Report on Integrating Media Literacy into the Secondary School Curriculum**

**Introduction**
This report examines the case for incorporating media literacy education into the secondary curriculum, identifies the core competencies students require, and sets out concrete recommendations for implementation. Evidence is drawn from research published by the Reuters Institute and pilot programmes conducted in three regional school authorities.

**Key Skills Required**
Students need to develop three interrelated competencies. First, source evaluation: the ability to assess the credibility, ownership and editorial stance of news outlets and online information providers. Second, critical analysis of visual and written content, including the capacity to identify logical fallacies, misleading statistics and emotionally manipulative framing. Third, an understanding of how algorithmic content curation shapes the information environment each user encounters.

**The Case for Early Intervention**
Research consistently demonstrates that information habits and heuristics established in adolescence tend to persist into adulthood. Introducing critical media skills before students are fully immersed in algorithmically curated social media environments gives educators the best opportunity to build resilient, sceptical information consumers.

**Recommendations**
Three measures are recommended: embedding a dedicated media literacy module within existing citizenship or English curricula for all Year 9 and Year 10 students; training subject teachers to incorporate source-evaluation exercises across disciplines; and partnering with established fact-checking organisations to provide up-to-date classroom resources.

**Conclusion**
Early, sustained media literacy education is one of the most cost-effective investments a society can make in the long-term health of its democratic institutions.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt: You are a member of a community journalism group. You have received an email from your local council asking for your group's views on a proposal to fund a public media literacy campaign targeting adult residents. Write an email to the council's communications director expressing your support for the initiative, suggesting two specific elements the campaign should include, and offering your group's assistance in delivering it.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Ms Holloway,

Thank you for consulting our group regarding the proposed public media literacy campaign. We welcome this initiative wholeheartedly and are delighted to share our recommendations.

Having worked within community journalism for several years, we have witnessed at first hand how misinformation spreads within our borough and the very real harm it causes — from health-related false claims shared on neighbourhood social media groups to fabricated local news stories that damage reputations and inflame tensions. A well-designed campaign has genuine potential to address these problems at a grassroots level.

We would urge the council to incorporate two elements in particular. First, a series of free, in-person workshops held at libraries, community centres and faith institutions that actively serve residents who are less likely to seek out information online. Our experience suggests that face-to-face engagement is considerably more effective than digital-only campaigns in reaching those most vulnerable to misinformation. Second, the campaign should provide residents with a simple, memorable checklist for evaluating online content — covering authorship, publication date, source credibility and the distinction between news reporting and opinion — which can be shared widely on printed materials as well as digitally.

We would be delighted to co-facilitate the workshop series, drawing on the expertise of our volunteer journalists and former editors. We are also happy to assist in developing the printed resources at no cost to the council.

Please do not hesitate to contact me if you would like to discuss this further.

Kind regards,
Patrick Osei
Media Literacy Lead, Northgate Community Journalism Group

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: You have recently watched a documentary called 'The Misinformation Machine' which investigates how fake news is produced, distributed and monetised online. Write a review for a current affairs magazine, evaluating the documentary's investigative depth, its effectiveness in explaining complex digital mechanisms to a general audience, and whether it offers any constructive solutions.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Illuminating but Unsettling: A Review of 'The Misinformation Machine'**

'The Misinformation Machine', the latest feature-length documentary from investigative filmmaker Rena Stavros, arrives at a moment when public understanding of the fake news ecosystem has never been more urgently needed. It is, for the most part, an admirably rigorous and deeply unsettling piece of work.

Stavros and her team spent eighteen months embedded within the fact-checking and platform-integrity communities, gaining access to researchers, former platform employees and, most remarkably, two individuals who operated large-scale misinformation websites for financial gain. These interviews are the documentary's most valuable contribution: they strip away any remaining notion that viral misinformation is primarily the product of ideologically motivated amateurs, revealing instead a sophisticated, monetised industry in which emotional manipulation is a deliberate and refined craft.

The documentary is equally strong in its explanation of algorithmic amplification for non-specialist viewers. Using clear visualisations and accessible analogies, Stavros demystifies the mechanisms by which engagement-optimising algorithms reward false and outrage-inducing content — a genuinely complex technical and economic dynamic that many previous attempts to explain have oversimplified or obscured.

Where the film is weaker is in its final act. Having built a compelling and damning case, it offers only a brief and somewhat vague exploration of potential remedies, gesturing towards regulation and education without engaging seriously with either. The problem has been diagnosed with precision; the prescription remains frustratingly underwritten.

Nevertheless, 'The Misinformation Machine' is essential and compulsively watchable journalism. Highly recommended.
